pronunciationTips.rule: In Belgian Dutch, much softer than Dutch NL — like a gentle rasp at the back of the throat, almost a breathy 'h'. e.g. goed ≈ 'khoot' (soft).
Sounds like English 'eye'. e.g. zijn ≈ 'zayn', tijd ≈ 'tayd'.
Tricky! Round your lips for 'oo', then say 'ee'. e.g. huis ≈ 'howss' (but rounder).
Like English 'oo' in 'food'. e.g. doen ≈ 'doon', goed ≈ 'khoot'.
Like French 'eu' or German 'ö'. Lips rounded, tongue forward. e.g. neus ≈ 'nöss'.
Long vowels — held slightly longer. aa='ah', ee='ay', oo='oh'.
Softer than English 'w' — almost between 'v' and 'w'. e.g. wij ≈ 'vay'.
In Belgian Dutch often pronounced as 'f' at the start of words. e.g. van ≈ 'fan'.
Rolled slightly at the back of throat (uvular). Softer than Spanish but more pronounced than English.
Final -n in words like 'maken' is often dropped in spoken Belgian Dutch: 'make' is how it sounds.
